current_adult_body:
Your brain files the day while you sleep. Skip it too often and things stop sticking — meetings, names, where you left your keys.

Consistent sleep beats one heroic ten-hour catch-up.

When you sleep, your brain sorts the day — a bit like tidying your room.

If you miss sleep a lot, remembering gets harder. A usual bedtime beats one huge catch-up night.

A dull wind-down routine is actually doing a lot of work.

A calm wind-down routine is doing real work, even if it feels boring.

Eating a big meal late at night tends to make blood-sugar control harder — even if the total calories are identical to an earlier meal.

It’s not about willpower. It’s just how the body prefers its schedule.

It is biology, not “being bad.”

You don’t need a full session. You just need to move.

Small links are more durable than big resolutions.

Tiny links beat giant promises.

current_adult_title: Why checking a box feels good

Your brain pays attention to better-than-expected outcomes. A small visible win — a tick, a streak — registers as one. That’s not shallow; it’s just how learning works.

Aim that wiring at things worth repeating.
current_kid_body:
Your brain likes little wins — checkmarks, stars, streaks.

Use that power for habits that help you.

Some brains genuinely struggle to feel how long things take without external cues. Calendars, alarms, and timers aren’t crutches — they’re just the right tool for that particular wiring.

Vague plans fail specific people. Specificity is the fix.

People who say “I’m someone who exercises” stick with it longer than people who say “I’m trying to exercise more.” Identity outruns motivation.

The story you tell on good days is the voice you hear on hard ones.

Tell a story that helps you on hard days.

Maintenance research keeps repeating one lesson: slips are normal; re-entry predicts long-term change more than a spotless streak.

Opening the log again after a rough week is already the behaviour scientists care about.
proposed_kid_body:
Slips are normal. Scientists care most that you return.

Opening the log after a rough week already counts.

current_adult_title: Habits added mid-week get a fair start
current_adult_body:
If you add a habit on a day other than Monday, that first partial week uses proportional targets — your threshold is scaled down based on how many days remain in the week.

For example, if your goal is 5 days per week and you start on Thursday, you only need to hit roughly 2 days (floor of 5 × 4/7). This keeps scoring fair without blocking your goal on a week you could never fully complete.
current_kid_body:
If you add a habit mid-week, your first partial week uses a smaller target so it stays fair.

Example: starting Thursday with a 5-day goal might ask ~2 days that week.

current_adult_body: Core habits must all qualify for a closed week to count toward your % and weekly treats; growth habits add pot money when they qualify but never block the week. In recovery (rolling % below that goal’s consistency floor), only core habits add £ to the pot; growth adds £0 until rolling % reaches the floor again. Each goal’s floor is set in Add/Edit goal (default often 80%).
current_kid_body:
Core habits are your must-do habits: when a week closes, they all need to qualify so the week counts on your score and for your treat.

Growth habits add jar money when you do them — they never block the week.

In recovery (below your floor), only core adds jar money until your score climbs back; growth waits. A grown-up can help with the numbers.

current_adult_body:
Popular books like “The Molecule of More” and “Dopamine Nation” stress one idea: much of what people call “reward” is wanting and momentum, not the cozy feeling after a good meal.

Chasing the next tick is dopamine-heavy; deep rest after is often other chemistry.

A lot of what people call “reward” is wanting and go-go energy, not only cosy afterglow.

Chasing the next tick uses different chemistry than deep rest.

sourceText: Lieberman & Long — The Molecule of More
current_adult_title: Two styles of dopamine, one you
current_adult_body:
Lieberman and Long describe a “here-and-now” chemical chorus and a “future-and-possible” dopamine track that loves novelty and goals — both matter.

Balance is the game: enough future-pull to plan, enough present chemistry to enjoy arriving.
current_kid_body:
Writers describe “here-and-now” chemistry and “future-and-maybe” chemistry — both matter.

Balance planning with enjoying the moment.

sourceText: Schultz et al.; Lieberman & Long (popular synthesis)
current_adult_body:
Dopamine-related circuits learn from surprises and progress signals — “better than expected” keeps attention. That is why visible streaks and near-complete bars tug harder than abstract goals.

You are not weak; you are using a loud channel wisely.
current_kid_body:
Brains learn from surprises and progress signals.

Visible streaks use a loud, honest channel — not a weakness.

current_adult_title: Pleasure and pain share a balance beam
current_adult_body:
Psychiatrist Anna Lembke argues endless easy hits — scrolls, snacks, buys — can tilt the brain toward needing more just to feel normal. Brief, chosen discomfort (effort, delay) resets the scale.

Habit apps that add friction on purpose echo that clinical lesson gently.
current_kid_body:
Endless quick hits — scrolls, snacks — can tilt the brain toward needing more to feel normal.

Chosen effort and breaks can reset the scale.

current_adult_title: Oxytocin is a trust hormone, not only a cuddle hormone
current_adult_body:
Studies tie oxytocin to bonding cues: touch, warm voice, shared meals. It nudges social calm and “we” feelings.

Accountability partners and family habits may ride social chemistry, not only willpower.
current_kid_body:
Oxytocin links to bonding cues: touch, warm voices, shared meals.

Family habits ride social chemistry, not only willpower.

current_adult_title: Oxytocin has more than one mood
current_adult_body:
Researchers note context matters: the same hormone that supports closeness can sharpen feelings about “us versus them” in some settings.

Kindness with boundaries stays wise — chemistry is not a morality tale.
current_kid_body:
The same hormone can nudge closeness or “us vs them” feelings depending on context.

Kindness plus boundaries stays wise.

current_adult_title: Serotonin is a stabiliser, not a smile pill
current_adult_body:
Most brain serotonin does not come with simple labels. It helps regulate mood, sleep timing, and even gut signalling — a steadying hand, not a happy switch.

Medication stories are separate from daily habits; both deserve nuance.
current_kid_body:
Serotonin helps mood, sleep timing, and even gut signals — a stabiliser, not a simple “happy switch.”

Medicine stories are separate from daily habits.

Exercise and outdoor light are linked to better mood and sleep in large studies — serotonin-related pathways are part of the story, alongside many other systems.

A walk is low-tech support for a complicated brain.

current_adult_body:
After intense pleasure or orgasm, prolactin often rises — part of why excitement eases and the body pivots toward rest. It is normal biology, not failure.

Understanding the cooldown helps you plan recovery, not judge it.
current_kid_body:
After very intense fun, the body shifts toward calm chemistry — that is normal biology.

Understanding cooldown helps you plan rest, not worry.

notes: Softened the explicit wording (dropped 'or orgasm') to match the app's calm, broadly-suitable tone.

current_adult_title: Prolactin shows up for caregivers too
current_adult_body:
Hormone research links prolactin to nurturing behaviours in many mammals — milk production in some contexts and caregiving tone more broadly.

Bodies reward tending others with quiet chemistry.
current_kid_body:
Hormone research ties prolactin to nurturing behaviours in many mammals.

Bodies quietly reward caring for others.

current_adult_body:
Sapolsky’s “Behave” walks one behaviour through genes, hormones, context, and culture — dopamine, serotonin, oxytocin, and more act in concert like instruments, not soloists.

When you change a habit, you are conducting the whole band slowly.

One behaviour involves genes, hormones, context, and culture together — like a band, not one instrument.

Changing a habit slowly conducts the whole band.

current_adult_title: Books beat doom-scrolling for the same circuits
current_adult_body:
Writers like Lembke and Lieberman describe how cheap digital hits train dopamine shortcuts. Swapping some scroll time for long-form explanations rebuilds patience — the same patience habits need.

This app’s slower rewards are a cousin of that swap.
current_kid_body:
Cheap digital hits train shortcuts; longer reading rebuilds patience.

This app’s slower rewards echo that gentler pace.

Research on habit formation shows “automaticity” emerges over weeks to months, with wide individual differences — not a single fixed number for everyone.

Earning this tier means your logged behaviour met the bar over roughly a month of calendar days for this habit — celebrate showing up.

As windows lengthen, off-days age out of the lookback while you keep accumulating proof you can return.

This tier is about sustaining the pattern on the Log across roughly two months of calendar days.

current_adult_title: Mastery III — you own this here
current_adult_body:
Meeting a high bar over the longest mastery window (calendar days) in this app is rare air — you have built real consistency with this habit here.

Consider banking it for now and revisiting in 6–12 months if you want to reinforce it again.
proposed_adult_title: Mastery III — you own this here

Meeting a high bar over the longest mastery window (calendar days) in this app is rare air — you have built real consistency with this habit here.

Consider banking it for now and revisiting in 6–12 months if you want to reinforce it again.

current_adult_body: A long pause after mastery is normal life. Choosing to return and meet the maintenance bar shows the habit is still yours — a deliberate reunion, not a rescue mission.
proposed_adult_body: A long pause after mastery is normal life. Choosing to return and meet the maintenance bar shows the habit is still yours — a deliberate reunion, not a rescue mission.

current_adult_body: Years later, the signal is not perfection. It is that you came back and let the weeks add up again — quiet proof that the identity stuck.
proposed_adult_body: Years later, the signal is not perfection. It is that you came back and let the weeks add up again — quiet proof that the identity stuck.

current_adult_body: Half a decade is enough seasons to fail, wander, and restart. Earning this means the pattern survived real life — not a streak fantasy.
proposed_adult_body: Half a decade is enough seasons to fail, wander, and restart. Earning this means the pattern survived real life — not a streak fantasy.

current_adult_body: A decade marker is as much about values as behaviour: some practices deserve to stay loosely tethered across everything else that changes.
proposed_adult_body: A decade marker is as much about values as behaviour: some practices deserve to stay loosely tethered across everything else that changes.

sourceText: Jean Piaget — developmental psychology; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds
current_adult_body:
For a small child, time has two settings: this moment, and everything else — which feels like infinity. That’s not immaturity. It’s how the brain is wired before it learns to feel the distance between today and next week.

Habits are how you train that distance to feel real.
proposed_adult_body:
For a small child, time has two settings: this moment, and everything else — which feels like infinity. That’s not immaturity. It’s how the brain is wired before it learns to feel the distance between today and next week.

Habits are how you train that distance to feel real.

sourceText: Jean Piaget; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds
current_adult_body:
The young brain defaults to magical thinking — want something hard enough and it arrives. Most adults still carry a version of this around goals.

The system you’re building here is the direct answer to that: small repeated actions, not intention.
proposed_adult_body:
The young brain defaults to magical thinking — want something hard enough and it arrives. Most adults still carry a version of this around goals.

The system you’re building here is the direct answer to that: small repeated actions, not intention.

sourceText: Jean Piaget — cognitive development; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds
current_adult_body:
No one is born understanding that today’s choices shape next week. That link — cause, then effect, across time — is something the brain constructs gradually. In some people that construction takes longer.

That’s not a deficit. It’s just the starting point.
proposed_adult_body:
No one is born understanding that today’s choices shape next week. That link — cause, then effect, across time — is something the brain constructs gradually. In some people that construction takes longer.

That’s not a deficit. It’s just the starting point.

sourceText: Jean Piaget — preoperational stage; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds
current_adult_body:
Piaget found that children don’t have a full grasp of time until around age seven. Before that, duration and consequence are genuinely hard to feel — not a behaviour choice.

For some brains, external structure does what that internal clock hasn’t fully built yet.
proposed_adult_body:
Piaget found that children don’t have a full grasp of time until around age seven. Before that, duration and consequence are genuinely hard to feel — not a behaviour choice.

For some brains, external structure does what that internal clock hasn’t fully built yet.

sourceText: Jean Piaget — preoperational stage; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds
current_adult_title: You can’t bend time by wanting to
current_adult_body:
Piaget observed that young children believe they can stop, speed up, or slow time down. That instinct doesn’t always fully leave. If deadlines feel like suggestions and schedules seem to shift around you — that’s the same wiring, older.

Structure isn’t a workaround. It’s the actual solution.
